segments.title: HMS Orion's wide-ranging wartime service
segments.summary: HMS Orion's battle honours trace a remarkably broad Royal Navy war, from the Atlantic in 1939 to Mediterranean and north-west European operations in 1944.
segments.body: Dr Clarke runs through the engagements attached to Orion's record, beginning with Atlantic service in 1939 and continuing through Calabria and the Mediterranean in 1940. Malta convoy duty, Matapan, Greece and Crete show how heavily the cruiser was used during the critical 1941 Mediterranean campaigns. He pauses over the curious absence of a general Mediterranean honour for 1941 even though those named operations place Orion squarely in the theatre. The later list moves through Sicily, Salerno, the Aegean, Anzio, Normandy and the south of France. Taken together, the honours present the Leander-class cruiser as a ship repeatedly committed across several of the Royal Navy's major wartime theatres.

evidence.note: The transcript lists Orion's Atlantic, Mediterranean, Greek, Italian, Aegean, Normandy and southern-France battle honours.

segments.title: Why battle-honour totals need context
segments.summary: A comparison with HMS Nubian, HMS Jervis and Warspite explains why battle-honour totals are not a simple ranking of a ship's wartime contribution.
segments.body: Orion is compared with HMS Nubian and HMS Jervis, while Warspite is said to sit ahead in the official count. Dr Clarke cautions that the categories reflect the type of ship as well as its service. A battleship's presence and gunfire could qualify for a battle honour, whereas cruisers and destroyers often had to meet a higher threshold for the same kind of recognition. Their campaign honours could therefore encompass several actions that might have been counted separately for a larger ship. The distinction makes Orion's long record more meaningful than a raw comparison of totals and supports the video's verdict that she was a war hero whose loss from preservation came too soon.

evidence.note: The transcript compares Orion with Nubian, Jervis and Warspite, then explains the different battle and campaign-honour treatment of battleships, cruisers and destroyers.
